verses.commentary: Not peace with ourselves or circumstances, but peace with God. The war is over; hostility has ended. Jesus Christ is the mediator of this peace.

Through whom we have gained access by faith into this grace in which we now stand. Access (prosagōgē, προσαγωγή) means introduction, like entering the king's presence. We stand in grace, not by our merit. And we boast in the hope of the glory of God. Hope is confident expectation. We will share in God's glory, the very thing we fell short of (3:23).
But suffering is not meaningless; it produces character. Perseverance (hypomonē, ὑπομονή) is endurance under trial.
ce, which forms character (dokimē, δοκιμή, proven quality), which strengthens hope. Trials refine and confirm faith.
The ground of hope is God's love poured out (perfect tense: already given and continuing) into our hearts by the Holy Spirit. The Spirit is the guarantee of future glory.
We were powerless (asthenēs, ἀσθενής, weak, unable). Christ died not for the righteous but for the ungodly. This is the scandal of grace.
Dying for a righteous or good person is rare. But Christ's love exceeds human love.
While we were still sinners, not after we cleaned up. This is love for enemies, the undeserving. The cross is the proof of God's love.
The logic is from the greater to the lesser. Justification guarantees final salvation.
Now that we are reconciled, salvation is certain. Christ's resurrection life secures our salvation.
Reconciliation (katallagē, καταλλαγή) is the restoration of relationship. We rejoice not in ourselves but in God.
Sin entered through Adam; death followed sin. Death spread to all because all sinned. Original sin: Adam's sin affects all his descendants.
This does not mean sin was not real, but it was not legally charged.
Even those who did not break an explicit command like Adam still died. Adam is a type (typos, τύπος) of Christ: one man's act affects many.
But Christ's work exceeds Adam's in power and scope. Grace overflows beyond the damage of sin.
But grace covers many sins and brings justification. Grace is greater than sin.
But those in Christ will reign in life. Not just escape death, but reign. Grace reverses the curse and elevates believers beyond Adam's original state.

Adam's one sin brought condemnation to all. Christ's one righteous act (His death and resurrection) brings justification and life to all who believe. All people means all in Adam or all in Christ, not universal salvation.
Christ's obedience constitutes many as righteous. Imputation works both ways: Adam's sin imputed to us, Christ's righteousness imputed to us.
But grace is greater than sin. Sin abounded, but grace superabounded (hyperperisseuō, ὑπερπερισσεύω). Grace always exceeds sin.
Grace reigns through righteousness (not despite it). The ground is Christ's work. The result is eternal life.
